step_by_step_reasoning: The transcript describes a build system on AWS EKS triggered by a GitLab instance (UserCompanyDeveloper). The system consists of dedicated GitLab Runners in an EKS Base Node Group, which spawn build job pods in an EKS Worker Node Group that auto-scales dynamically (0 to 50 nodes). The pods access build caches and artifacts stored in Amazon S3 via a VPC endpoint to optimize performance, before uploading new artifacts and reporting completion back to the runner, which updates GitLab.

graph.name: Age of Learning's Scalable EKS Build Architecture
graph.notes: Migrated from 150 individual VMs to an autoscaling EKS-based GitLab Runner architecture. Handles 20k to 25k builds per month, yielding 50-60% faster build times and significant cost savings via spot instances and S3 gateway caching.

nodes.name: GitLab CI Platform
nodes.notes: Primary GitLab instance where developers commit code and trigger CI/CD builds.

nodes.name: GitLab Runners (Base Node Group)
nodes.notes: Dedicated, always-on base node group running GitLab runners to queue and coordinate incoming build requests.

nodes.name: Build Jobs (Worker Node Group)
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Dynamically scales from 0 to 50 instances utilizing Spot instances (.16xlarge and .metal) in multiple AZ subnets to run Kubernetes build pods.

nodes.name: Artifact and Cache S3 Bucket
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Stores cache and build artifacts up to 50GB in size. Accessed via VPC endpoint to achieve high throughput and 50-60% build speedups.

edges.notes: GitLab triggers runner to start build.
Runner schedules and spins up a Kubernetes job pod on the worker node group.
Job pod queries S3 for existing cache/artifacts.
S3 returns cached items to speed up compilation and scanning.
Completed job uploads fresh build cache/artifacts back to S3.
Job reports build completion status back to the coordinator runner.
Runner passes build results back to primary GitLab instance.
